explicit_text: I was born in Atlanta in 1902, just a few blocks from the Biltmore. Growing up in Atlanta was important to me, especially since my grandfather, Evan Howell, was the founder and publisher of the Atlanta newspaper. My early years in Atlanta shaped much of my perspective, even though I never went into the newspaper business like some of my family. Later, after my studies, I returned to Atlanta to work with the Commission on Interracial Cooperation.

explicit_text: I was born and raised in Georgia, specifically in Atlanta, and attended public schools there before going to the University of Georgia. My family had deep roots in the state, with my grandfather on my father's side being a farmer in Washington, Georgia. My experiences in Georgia, including witnessing racial violence near Athens, deeply influenced my views and later work in racial cooperation.

explicit_text: I attended the University of Georgia starting around 1916 or 1917 and graduated in 1921 at the age of 19. My experience there was fairly ordinary academically, focusing on liberal arts like Latin and Greek. However, a pivotal moment was living at the Chi Phi fraternity house in Athens during a lynching incident, which profoundly affected me and my views on race and justice.

explicit_text: Athens, Georgia, was the site of a brutal lynching that I witnessed indirectly while at the University of Georgia. The town was proud of its 'mob-proof' courthouse, yet a mob still gathered and burned a man to death outside the city. This event left a lasting impression on me and influenced my later commitment to racial justice.

explicit_text: While at Harvard in 1921-22, I was invited by Donald Culross Peattie to hear W. E. B. Du Bois speak at the Liberal Club. Du Bois made a great impression on me with his speech, marking my first significant break from the southern racial traditions I had grown up with. This experience was an important step in my evolving views on race and equality.

Harvard was a place where I began to question southern traditions and expand my worldview before traveling to Europe.

explicit_text: Before traveling to Europe, my mother gave me a book by H. G. Wells, which I read on the boat to London. I was greatly impressed by his vision of a better society, especially the last chapter. I even managed to meet him in London, where he advised me on learning languages and studying at the London School of Economics, shaping my educational path.

Oldham's book about the Commission on Interracial Cooperation, which inspired me to return to Atlanta and work for racial justice.

His book detailed the founding and work of the Commission on Interracial Cooperation in Atlanta, which I had never heard of before. This reading motivated me to return home and work for the Commission, marking a turning point in my career.

explicit_text: After reading about the Commission on Interracial Cooperation in J. H. Oldham's book, I decided to return to Atlanta to work for it. My father arranged for me to meet Dr. Ashby Jones, the head, and then Will Alexander, the director. Although initially unpaid, I soon received a salary and was proud to contribute to racial cooperation efforts in my hometown.
